food_name: Spaghetti with Meatballs in Tomato Sauce
aliases: Pasta with Meatballs in Tomato Sauce

english: Pasta with Meatballs in Tomato Sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for spaghetti with meatballs in tomato sauce; closest parent 'pasta food product' (FOODON_00002473) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Pasta, cooked) used as closest parent; no specific code exists for spaghetti with meatballs and tomato sauce as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 'Pasta dish (food)' used as closest parent; no specific term for spaghetti with meatballs.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of spaghetti with meatballs in tomato sauce is approximately 400 g, comprising cooked spaghetti, three to four beef meatballs, tomato sauce, and a garnish of grated parmesan cheese.

nutrient_source.title: Spaghetti with meat sauce, homemade-style — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry covers spaghetti with beef meatballs and tomato sauce as a composite dish. Values estimated by proportional combination of USDA SR Legacy entries: cooked spaghetti (FDC ID 20124), beef meatballs (FDC ID 13357 ground beef cooked, plus egg and breadcrumb binder), and tomato-based pasta sauce (FDC ID 11549), with parmesan cheese (FDC ID 1017). Approximate dish composition assumed: 45% cooked spaghetti, 35% beef meatballs, 18% tomato sauce, 2% parmesan. Values are estimates and should be verified against a direct composite analysis.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 45 assigned based on cooked spaghetti (al dente) GI values reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), where spaghetti cooked al dente has a GI of approximately 40–49 (mean ~45). The addition of beef meatballs and tomato sauce lowers the overall meal GI relative to plain pasta due to protein and fat co-ingestion. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 63.2) / 100 = 28.4, using 400 g serving with 15.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 1240 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation, limit portion size to 200–250 g, and avoid additional table salt or high-sodium condiments such as parmesan in excess.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index of 45 (low GI) but high glycemic load per standard serving (~28.4) due to large carbohydrate quantity in a restaurant-sized portion.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to approximately 200 g to keep glycemic load manageable, choose al dente cooking to preserve lower GI, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to slow glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef meatballs and parmesan cheese contributes approximately 1.9 g saturated fat per 100 g; a full 400 g serving provides approximately 7.6 g saturated fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should opt for leaner meatball formulations (e.g., turkey or chicken), reduce parmesan quantity, and limit portion size to control saturated fat and total caloric intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spaghetti with Meatballs in Tomato Sauce (Italian: Spaghetti e Polpette al Sugo) is a classic Italian-American pasta dish consisting of long spaghetti noodles served with homemade beef meatballs simmered in a rich Italian tomato sauce and finished with grated Parmesan cheese. The spaghetti is boiled until al dente, preserving a firm texture and a lower glycemic index compared to overcooked pasta. The meatballs are typically made from ground beef mixed with breadcrumbs, egg, garlic, and herbs, then pan-fried or baked before being simmered in the tomato sauce to absorb flavour. The tomato sauce is prepared with crushed tomatoes, olive oil, garlic, onion, basil, and oregano. The dish has a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile with mild acidity from the tomatoes and a hearty, satisfying texture. Nutritionally, it provides a balanced macronutrient profile with moderate protein from the beef meatballs, complex carbohydrates from the pasta, and fat from the meat and cheese. The dish is a significant source of iron, zinc, vitamin B12, and lycopene from the tomato sauce. Clinically, the sodium content is a concern for hypertension patients, and the glycemic load per restaurant serving is moderate to high for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. In Singapore, this dish is served at Italian restaurants such as Ricciotti at The Riverwalk and is priced at the premium end of the casual dining segment.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source covers this exact composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by proportional combination of USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked spaghetti, cooked ground beef, tomato pasta sauce, and parmesan cheese. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database values for al dente spaghetti. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality USDA SR Legacy entries and the GI source is peer-reviewed.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this composite estimate. All other -1 fields have been replaced with estimated values from USDA SR Legacy component data. Nutrient values represent a weighted composite estimate based on assumed dish composition of 45% cooked spaghetti, 35% beef meatballs, 18% tomato sauce, and 2% parmesan cheese; actual restaurant preparation at Ricciotti may differ. Glycemic load per serving calculated using 400 g serving size and 15.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g: GL = (45 × 63.2) / 100 = 28.4. This dish is not a Singaporean dish; multilingual fields for Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are not applicable and left null. Chinese names provided as this dish is served in Singapore and Chinese-language retrieval is relevant. The cuisine_tag 'Chicken Dish' in the source input appears to be a data error from the field survey platform and has been disregarded.
